vocabulary.exampleMeaning: I think so / I think yes.

hook.text: Russian has two conjugation classes — first and second. Today you learn the first. The pattern is systematic: one set of six endings attaches to the verb stem and tells you who's doing the action. Once you know the endings, you can conjugate hundreds of verbs. No exceptions in this class — just the pattern.

why.text: Unlike English, Russian can drop the subject pronoun because the verb ending already shows who's acting. Читаю means 'I read' without needing я. But you do need the ending to be correct — get it wrong and Russian speakers will struggle to understand who's doing what.

cards.title: Personal pronouns — all six
cards.body: Russian has six grammatical persons, matching six conjugation endings. You know я and ты. Now the full set: он/она/оно (he/she/it), мы (we), вы (you formal/plural), они (they).

cards.title: First conjugation endings
cards.body: Find the stem: remove -ТЬ from the infinitive (читать → чита-). Add the endings. For е-verbs, the endings use the vowel Е (or Ю/Ю for я/они).

cards.title: The stem-change verbs
cards.body: Some first-conjugation verbs have a consonant change in the stem. писать is the key example: the С changes to Ш in conjugation. This pattern recurs — when you see an irregular stem, it's usually a consonant mutation, not a completely different paradigm.

questions.prompt: Conjugate знать for 'мы'. What is 'we know'?
questions.explanation: знать → stem зна- + -ем = знаем. мы знаем = we know.

questions.prompt: What ending does ты take in first conjugation?
questions.explanation: ты → -ешь. So: ты знаешь, ты читаешь, ты думаешь.
писать has a stem-change: С→Ш. я + -у = пишу.

cards.title: Russian present tense = English present + progressive
cards.body: Russian has no separate progressive form. Я читаю means both 'I read' (habitually) and 'I am reading' (right now). Context decides which meaning. Don't try to add a separate form for 'I am reading' — it doesn't exist in Russian.

examples.meaning: I read a book. / I am reading a book.

cards.title: First conjugation — the full endings table
cards.body: Memorise this. Every first-conjugation verb uses these six endings: -Ю, -ЕШЬ, -ЕТ, -ЕМ, -ЕТЕ, -ЮТ. Note: after stems ending in a vowel, it's -Ю/-ЮТ. After consonants with spelling rules, the Е may change. But the pattern holds.

questions.prompt: Conjugate думать for 'она'. What is 'she thinks'?
questions.explanation: думать → stem дума- + -ет = думает. она думает = she thinks.
понимать → stem понима- + -ешь = понимаешь. ты понимаешь.
читать → stem чита- + -ют = читают. они читают.

questions.prompt: What is the ending for он/она in first conjugation?
questions.explanation: он/она takes -ет in first conjugation: знает, читает, думает.
знать → stem зна- + -ю = знаю. я знаю.
Russian present tense covers both. Context determines which English translation fits.

rules: Stem = infinitive minus -ТЬ (читать → чита-, знать → зна-)
Russian present = English present + present progressive — no separate форм
Some verbs have consonant stem changes (писать: С→Ш before endings)
Pronouns: я ты он/она/оно мы вы они
summary.nextLesson: Next: second conjugation — и-verbs like говорить and учить, with their own set of six endings.

questions.prompt: What is 'я know' — conjugate знать for я?
What is 'she reads' — conjugate читать for она?
What is the ты ending in first conjugation?

options: Both I read and I am reading
questions.answer: Both I read and I am reading
